summary: The 221 direct registrations through the atexit wrapper at 0x1407B8460 are now clustered by callback body profile.

All 221 registrations resolve to unique .text callback pointers from the pe-callsite-registers inventory. 166 callbacks have normal .pdata runtime-function coverage, while 55 are tiny leaf callbacks or thunks without .pdata entries.

The 55 no-.pdata callbacks split into 49 simple LEA/JMP object thunks and 6 custom leaf bodies. These are mostly destructor-routing glue and should be treated as shutdown-noise wrappers rather than primary bootstrap/client subsystems.

The largest no-.pdata family contains 33 callbacks shaped as LEA RCX, [global_object]; JMP 0x1407B51B0. The shared target at 0x1407B51B0 adjusts RCX by +8, loads a vtable slot at +0x20, then jumps through the CFG dispatch pointer at 0x140803060. This is best read as a virtual destructor dispatch thunk for static/global objects whose destructible subobject begins at +8.

The .pdata-backed callbacks are much more diverse: 166 callbacks produce 148 call/literal signatures. The largest .pdata cluster is 17 no-call callbacks, followed by small repeated destructor families that call 0x1400B0D90, 0x14002D100, 0x14042B500, 0x1405A5180, 0x1400DFF10, 0x1402F0BE0, and 0x1403C4260.

No callback body in this pass produced a string literal signature, which reinforces that this layer is mostly static object teardown and library/runtime cleanup, not protocol parsing, JS5, or login-key logic.

dominant_leaf_families.shared_target_behavior: Adds 8 to RCX, loads [RCX]->[+0x20], and jumps through CFG dispatch 0x140803060.
Checks object fields at +0x10/+0x8 and routes release through the process-global manager at 0x140E57B40 plus an indirect virtual call.
Checks a high flag bit at object+0x17, validates a payload pointer, then uses process-global manager 0x140E57B40 for indirect release work.
Both targets check object payload bounds and route release work through process-global manager 0x140E57B40.

named_or_high_signal_callbacks.reason: Calls KERNEL32!SetUnhandledExceptionFilter and cleanup helpers; this is process exception-filter/CRT shutdown scaffolding.
Calls KERNEL32!SuspendThread and KERNEL32!TerminateThread; this is thread shutdown cleanup.
Deletes critical section 0x140C56030 and closes fallback event 0x140C56028; condition-variable compatibility teardown.
Walks empty/reserved terminator table 0x140BB22B8..0x140BB22B8 via CFG dispatch pointer 0x140803060.
Tiny leaf body resets 0x140C36DF8 to 0x140B64F98 and returns; likely default global state restoration.
Pure RET 0 stub; registered callback is inert.

tooling_notes: pe-callsite-registers was the key reusable tool for this milestone: it recovered every RCX callback pointer for the 0x1407B8460 wrapper without raw-byte false positives.

pe-runtime-functions alone is not sufficient for this callback layer because 55 callbacks are valid tiny leaf thunks with no .pdata record. For these, pe-instructions windows are necessary to classify the LEA/JMP body.

Some shared leaf targets are also split into very small .pdata fragments, so exact instruction-window evidence is more reliable than treating every runtime-function boundary as a semantic function boundary.
bounded_inference: This milestone does not name all 221 destructors. It does establish that the dynamic atexit inventory is dominated by static/global object teardown and CRT/library cleanup. The currently mapped layer is therefore useful mostly as a pruning map: future bootstrap, JS5, and login-key work should deprioritize these teardown-only signatures unless one is directly referenced from startup state construction.
next_steps: Use the cluster map to exclude shutdown-only callbacks from bootstrap path exploration.
Follow startup root 0x1407B8480 past CRT setup into the first non-CRT client initialization branches.
If more wrapper families appear, reuse pe-callsite-registers to recover static RCX/RDX/R8/R9 arguments before clustering bodies.
